coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence enhanced probe for the determination of glucose
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yuxuan
Wen, Jialin
Li, Dan
Li, Yuting
Alheshibri, Muidh
Zhang, Minmin
Shui, Lingling
Li, Na
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for the detection of H2O2 and glucose was developed based on green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The CDs was newly prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method with Eosin Y and branched polyethylenimine as precursors. Interestingly, in the presence of H2O2 and HRP, the fluorescence of the CDs enhanced significantly with a red-shift emission due to their “aggregation”. Meanwhile, the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase could generate H2O2. Thus, a simple sensing system based on the CDs as fluorescent probes was constructed for H2O2 and glucose determination, avoiding the fluorescence quenching and subsequent recovery process in conventional turn-on strategy. The method showed good selectivity and sensitivity for glucose sensing with the detection limit of 0.12 μM. The method was further applied to glucose detection in real samples. The obtained results demonstrated the simplicity, selectivity and practicality of the method. This work expands the carbon nanomaterials with fluorescence emission enhancement properties. It provides a new and direct “turn-on” strategy for H2O2 and glucose detection, which could be a simple and effective tool for screening biological substances involved in H2O2-generation reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence emission enhancement
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose
Turn-on

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the synthesized CDs and their particle size distribution (Top inset).
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) full-scan XPS spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectrum of (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s and (f) Br 3d of the CDs.

(a) UV − vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths. Effect of pH value (c) and NaCl (d) on the fluorescence of the CDs.

(a) fluorescence spectra of the CDs with the coexistence of different compounds and (b) correponding fluorescence intensities of these solutions; (c) UV–Vis spectra of the CDs with the coexistence of different compounds; (d) fluorescence lifetime of the CDs in the absence and presence of H2O2 and HRP.

(a)Fluorescence spectra of the system with adding different concentrations of H2O2 and (b) plots of fluorescence enhancement degree versus H2O2 concentration. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the system with adding different concentrations of glucose and (d) plots of fluorescence enhancement degree versus glucose concentration. The excitation and emission wavelengths for H2O2/glucose detection were 500 nm and 526 nm, respectively.
Selectivity of this sensing system for glucose determination. The concentrations of the interferents were 0.5 mM and the concentration of glucose was 0.05 mM.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence turn-on detection of H2O2 and glucose based on green fluorescent CDs.
Comparison of the analytical performance of this method and others works based on CDs for fluorescence turn-on detection of glucose.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence enhanced probe for the determination of glucose

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for the detection of H2O2 and glucose was developed based on green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The CDs was newly prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method with Eosin Y and branched polyethylenimine as precursors. Interestingly, in the presence of H2O2 and HRP, the fluorescence of the CDs enhanced significantly with a red-shift emission due to their “aggregation”. Meanwhile, the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase could generate H2O2. Thus, a simple sensing system based on the CDs as fluorescent probes was constructed for H2O2 and glucose determination, avoiding the fluorescence quenching and subsequent recovery process in conventional turn-on strategy. The method showed good selectivity and sensitivity for glucose sensing with the detection limit of 0.12 μM. The method was further applied to glucose detection in real samples. The obtained results demonstrated the simplicity, selectivity and practicality of the method. This work expands the carbon nanomaterials with fluorescence emission enhancement properties. It provides a new and direct “turn-on” strategy for H2O2 and glucose detection, which could be a simple and effective tool for screening biological substances involved in H2O2-generation reaction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The level of biologically active molecules in living organisms is closely related to health status of the organism. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an essential biochemical molecular, which plays an important role in adjusting various biological processes such as aging and carcinogenesis [1,2]. Additionally, H2O2 is the main product of a variety of enzymatic reactions, such as H2O2-generation from glucose oxidation via the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOx). Glucose is main energy source for cell activity and metabolism in human body and is recognized as an important indicator of human health. Abnormal levels of glucose usually associate with some disease, such as the most widespread chronic disease of diabetes mellitus, hypoglycaemia and some cancers [3–5]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose is of great significance for biomedical research, clinical monitoring and clinical diagnosis.

Fluorescence-based methods are widely applied to biochemical molecular detection owing to the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, easy operation, abundantly available fluorophores and accessible instruments [6,7]. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), as novel zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterisals show great potentials in fluorescence sensing due to ease of synthesis, photostability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, water dispersibility and abundant raw materials [7,8]. The fluorescence methods based on CDs have been used to determine various analytes (i.e. pH, metal ions, biomolecular and bioenzyme activity) [9–12]. Among these methods, fluorescence “turn-off” and “turn-on” modes were most widely used. In fluorescence turn-off strategy, the analytes usually act as quencher and trigger the fluorescence quenching of the CDs, accompanying with the decrease of fluorescence brightness. However, “turn-off” mode is usually with low sensitivity and selectivity, and easy to produce false positive signal. By contrast, the fluorescence turn-on mode is based on the analytes-triggered fluorescence enhancement and usually provides higher sensitivity and selectivity [13]. In “turn-on” mode, the fluorescence “switch-on” strategy termed as turn on–off-on is commonly used, in which the quenchers are firstly used to turn off the fluorescence of CDs, and the analytes are added into the sensing system to induce the removal of quenching effect and promote the fluorescence “turn-on” of CDs again [13–20]. Additionally, the redox reactions between the quencher and the analytes (or their related product) are usually involved for removing quenching effect. A fluorescence turn-on system based on CDs/Fe3+ was constructed for ascorbic acid detection [14]. Fe3+ could effectively quench the blue fluorescence of the CDs due to electron transfer mechanism, and the addition of ascorbic acid could reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ and recovery the blue fluorescence. Li et al. constructed a turn-on fluorescence assay based on CDs and MnO2 nanosheets for glucose detection with the detection limit of 0.83 μM [15]. MnO2 nanosheets can effectively quench the CDs’ fluorescence due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism·H2O2 generated from GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose could reduce MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ and restore the CDs fluorescence. With the similar principle, the sensing systems combined CDs with Ti3C2 or silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were constructed for glucose detection [16,18]. In “switch-on” strategy, the fluorescence recovery degree of the fluorophores usually have a great effect on the sensing performance. Recently, a more simple “turn-on” strategy attracts much attention, which is based on the fluorescence emission enhancement properties of some special CDs triggered by the specific compounds. 2013, Gao et al. firstly found a kind of CDs prepared from C60 displayed a nanoparticle aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) in the presence of adenosine-5-triphosphate [21], differing from the conventional AIEE effect resulted from fluorescent organic small molecules. 2014, boron-doped graphene quantum dots was prepared and used for the fluorescence turn-on detection of glucose, in which the reaction between the two cis-diol units of glucose and the boronic acid groups on the CDs surface could lead to the “aggregation” of the CDs and their photoluminescence enhancement [22]. Progesterone induced emission enhancement was reported for yellow-green emissive CDs, accompanying with blue-shift in emission maxima [23]. Red fluorescent CDs, prepared with neutral red and thiourea as precursors, displayed an obvious emission enhancement with adding tetracycline antibiotics [24]. Nitrogen-doped CDs prepared from adenine and ethylenediamine exhibited fluorescence enhancement behavior in the presence of hypochlorite [25]. In addition, metal ions induced emission enhancement of the CDs are observed [26,27]. The CDs with fluorescence emission enhancement properties provided a simple and effective tool to improve emission intensity and analytical performance. However, a few kinds of CDs showed the special emission enhancement property in the presence of specific targets. The direct fluorescence enhancement of CDs induced by H2O2 has not been reported. The further exploration of this kind of CDs is of great significance to the fluorescent CDs research and their applications in biochemical analysis field.

In this work, a new kind of green fluorescent CDs was prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method with Eosin Y and branched polyethylenimine as precursors. It is interesting that the fluorescence of as-prepared CDs enhances significantly accompanying with a red-shift emission in the presence of H2O2 and horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Meanwhile, the glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx could generate H2O2 and glucose quantification could be indirectly realized by H2O2 quantification. Thus, a novel and simple “turn-on” fluorescent sensor was developed for the detection of H2O2 and glucose (Scheme 1 ), in which the direct fluorescence enhancement of the CDs induced by H2O2 was realized and avoided the complex process of fluorescence quenching and subsequent recovery in conventional “switch-on” mode. The possible sensing mechanism was investigated. The effects of some key experimental parameters were investigated. The sensing system was further applied to glucose detection in real serum samples. Good results demonstrated the present method provides a simple, sensitive and selective way for H2O2 and glucose determination.

Eosin Y disodium salt, branched polyethylenimine (M.W. of 800, bPEI), horseradish peroxidase (HRP, ≥ 300 U/mg), D-galactose, ascorbic acid (AA), urea, sodium chloride (NaCl) and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. D-glucose, D-fructose and D-sucrose were purchased from Beijing Baring Technology Co. Glucose oxidase (GOx) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Lycine and tyrosine were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) was purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. The other inorganic salts used were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. The phosphate buffer solution (PB) of different pH was prepared by sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4). Healthy human serum samples were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science-Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water (DI, 18.25 MΩ·cm) was obtained by Milli-Q ultra-pure water system.

The morphology and composition of the CDs were tested by JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, USA), and AXIS SUPRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Shimadzu, Japan) excited by monochromatic X-ray source (Al Kα, 1486.6 eV). Fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were recorded by F-4600 fluorophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV2600 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Fluorescence lifetime was obtained by Fluorolog-3 HORIBA Jobin Yvon GmbH fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 500 nm, Germany). PB-10 pH meter (Sedorius, Germany) and BPG-9 106B blast drying oven (Bluepard, China) were used.

A new kind of CDs was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method referred to the literature [28]. 1 mL bPEI, 10.1 mg Eosin Y disodium salt were dissolved in 4 mL DI water. The mixture was mixed and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined, stainless steel autoclave, which were placed in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was dialyzed with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da against ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, the purified CDs (11 mg/mL) were stored at 4 °C in the dark.

The procedure for H2O2 detection as follows: 50 μL HRP (0.1 mg/mL), 380 μL PB (200 mM, pH = 7.4), 200 μL H2O2 and 70 μL CDs solution (diluted 20 times) were added to the centrifuge tube (1.5 mL) in turn. The above solution was fully mixed and reacted in the dark at room temperature for 40 min. After that, the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the solution was measured at λex = 500 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity at 526 nm was used for H2O2 quantification.

The procedure for glucose detection was similar to that of H2O2 detection. 100 μL GOx (0.1 mg/mL), 50 μL HRP (0.1 mg/mL), 280 μL PB (200 mM, pH = 7.4), 200 μL glucose and 70 μL diluted CDs solution were successively added to the centrifuge tube, mixed and reacted at room temperature. Finally, the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the solution was collected. The excitation and emission wavelengths for glucose detection were 500 nm and 526 nm, respectively.

The morphology of the synthesized CDs was characterized by TEM. In Fig. 1 , the CDs dispersed uniformly with diameters of ca. 3–7 nm and the average diameter of the CDs was 4.9 ± 0.94 nm with counting 138 of nanoparticles. Most of the CDs are amorphous without detectable lattice fringes, which indicates the internal structure of the CDs should be a crosslinked polymer network.

The surface functional groups of the CDs were characterized by FT-IR. In Fig. 2
a, the characteristic vibrations of N–H (3275 and 1590 cm−1) were observed. The absorption bands at 3344 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H. The absorption peaks at 2935 and 2819 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of CH2 bonds. The absorption peak at 1648 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibration of C = O bonds. The absorption peak at 1458 cm−1 and 1345 were attributed to the vibrations of C-N. The peaks corresponding to the stretching vibration of C-O is located at 1119 cm−1. The absorption bands within 600–900 cm−1 are caused by the bending vibration of aromatic rings.

XPS characterization was used to study the chemical composition and bonding of the CDs. In the full-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 b), the strong signal of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s and a weak signal of Br 3d were observed, indicating the CDs consist of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and bromine elements. The C 1 s band revealed the presence of four type carbon, including C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C = O (287.7 eV), C-O (286.1 eV) and C-N (285.4 eV) units (Fig. 2 c) [29]. In Fig. 2 d, the N1s band exhibited two peaks centered at 398.8 eV and 399.4 eV, corresponding to C-N and N–H units, respectively [28]. The O 1 s band presents three peaks of C-O-C/C-OH, C = O and O = C-OH, respectively (Fig. 2 e) [30]. The Br 3d band displayed two peaks centered at 67.5 eV and 70.5 eV, assigned to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 (Fig. 2 f). The results indicated the surface of the CDs probably contains hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which is consistent with FT-IR analysis.

The optical properties of the CDs were studied by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 ). In UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 3 a), two absorption bands at around 300 nm and 490 nm were observed, which were probably attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated C = C and n-π* transition of C = O/C = N bonds, respectively [24]. The CDs solution presented orange color under daylight and emitted intense green fluorescence under UV light (inset in Fig. 3 a). The optimal excitation and emission occurred at ∼ 490 nm and ∼ 519 nm. The CDs exhibited nearly excitation-independent photoluminescence in green region (Fig. 2 b). The relative quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs was calculated to be 2.74% with quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution) as the reference.

The effect of pH value on the fluorescence of the CDs were investigated. The pH value of the CDs solution was adjusted by PB buffer solutions. With the increase of pH value from 2 to 7, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs clearly increased, accompanying with a clear blue-shift emission (Fig. 3 c and Figure S1 ). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs changed slightly in alkaline conditions and the corresponding emission peak shifted slightly. The blue-shift emission of the CDs with pH increase is probably related to the deprotonation degree of surface functional groups. The fluorescence of the CDs remains stable with adding NaCl concentration from 0 to 100 mM, indicating good stability of the CDs in high ionic strength conditions (Fig. 3 d). In addition, the CDs showed good stability in the temperature ranged from 20 to 55 ℃ ( Figure S2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms for sensing H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The possible sensing mechanism was investigated. In UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 4 c), the absorption band of the CDs at around 490 nm was red shifted with the addition of H2O2 and HRP, suggesting the increased π-conjugation degree of the CDs [29]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs decreased from 3.57 ns to 2.47 ns in the presence of H2O2 and HRP (Fig. 4 d), indicating the change of radiative transition process. In addition, fluorescence emission decay curve and the lifetime of the CDs (2.54 ns) with adding HRP were similar to those of the mixture of CDs, H2O2 and HRP, suggesting the same radiative transition process. In TEM images ( Figure S5 ), the particle sizes of the CDs increased with the addition of H2O2 and HRP, indicating the “aggregation” of the CDs. Based on the results, the fluorescence enhancement of the CDs probably caused by the oxidation of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, and the crosslinking and aggregation of the CDs. In the presence of H2O2 and HRP, oxidative species such as radical intermediates generated in the system could result in the oxidation of surface groups on the CDs (i.e. hydroxyl or amino groups) and increase of interactive bonds and crosslinking [25,31]. Thus, the π-conjugation was extended and the “aggregation” of the CDs by π-π stacking occurred, which could promote radiative transitions, suppress non-radiative transitions and enhance the CDs fluorescence [25,32].

The effects of reaction time and GOx concentration on glucose detection were also studied. The reaction time was set at 60 min and 100 μg/mL of GOx was used in subsequent experiments ( Figure S8 ). Glucose solution with different concentration were analyzed. The fluorescence intensity increased gradually with increasing glucose concentration from 0 to 50 μM and changed slightly with further glucose addition (Fig. 5 c and 5d). The obtained regression equation was Y = 148.7Cglucose + 12.24 (R2 = 0.9910) with a linear range of 0.2–6 μM (inset of Fig. 5
d). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the 3σ/k definition (σ means the standard deviation of blank measurement and k is the slope of calibration curve). The LOD was 0.12 μM for glucose detection in this work, which is lower or comparable to those obtained by other methods for fluorescence turn-on glucose detection (Table 1 ) [15–18,22,34,35]. These methods mainly belong to “switch-on” strategy, in which the complex process of fluorescence quenching and subsequent recovery are performed and the fluorescence recovery of the fluorophores usually are lossy resulted in decreased sensitivity. In this method, the fluorescence enhancement of the CDs induced by H2O2 was realized in one step, which provides a simpler and more sensitive way for detecting H2O2 and biological substances involved in H2O2-generation reaction. However, compared with other methods, the method showed the deficiency of narrow linear range, which limited its application in broad range analysis.

To investigate the potential practicability, the present method was applied to glucose detection in real human serum. Four samples were analyzed and good correlation coefficient (r = 0.9917) was obtained between the results obtained by this method and a commercial glucometer ( Figure S10
and
Table S1 ). The precision and accuracy of this method were evaluated by the RSDs and recovery, respectively. The RSDs (n = 3) were ranged from 5.2% to 11.5% for the analysis of four real serum samples. Meanwhile, the spiked serum samples with spiked concentration of 2.5 mM were also analyzed with the recoveries ranging from 80.9% to 106.1% and the RSDs (n = 3) ranging from 5.3% to 9.3%. The results indicated the precision and accuracy of this method is acceptable. In addition, the precision and accuracy obtained by this method is comparable or slightly lower than those obtained by other methods in the analysis of real serum samples ( Table S2 ) [16,18,34]. Besides high sensitivity and selectivity, the results demonstrated the potential applicability of this strategy in biological samples.

In this work, a novel and simple “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on the CDs was constructed for H2O2 and glucose determination. The green fluorescent CDs was newly prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The CDs exhibited a remarkable fluorescence enhancement in the presence of H2O2 and HRP, probably due to the oxidation of surface groups of the CDs and the resultant crosslinking and aggregation of the CDs. Combining GOx-catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to produce H2O2, the method was used for glucose detection. The present method showed good selectivity and sensitivity for glucose detection with the LOD of 0.12 μM. The obtained results in real serum samples analysis demonstrated the practical applicability of this method. Therefore, this work provides a simple and effective method for determining biological metabolites related to H2O2-generation and showed promising potential in clinical diagnosis and research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Guangdong Province Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2021A1515110236), the International-Hong Kong-Macao-Taiwan Top Talents Exchange Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2022A0505020004), the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (No. 202201010248), the Young Scholar Foundation of South China Normal University (21KJ08), the Youth Innovation Project of Guangdong Education Department (2020KQNCX018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123149.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Biosens. Bioelectron.
